summary:
The Beer Hall Putsch was a failed coup attempt by the National Socialist German Workers' Party (Nazi Party) in Munich on the night of November 8–9, 1923. Adolf Hitler, then leader of the small but growing Nazi Party, attempted to seize power in Bavaria as a launching point for a national revolution against the [[event:2756|Weimar Republic]]. Hitler and approximately 600 armed SA stormtroopers interrupted a meeting of Bavarian government officials in the Bürgerbräukeller beer hall, taking the officials briefly hostage and announcing the revolution. The following morning, a march of 2,000 Nazis through Munich was broken up by police gunfire, killing sixteen marchers and four police officers. Hitler was arrested and charged with treason.

At his trial, Hitler used the proceedings as a political platform, delivering lengthy speeches that attracted national press coverage and transformed him from a regional agitator into a figure of national recognition. Convicted of treason in April 1924, he was sentenced to five years but was released after less than nine months, during which he dictated Mein Kampf to his secretary Rudolf Hess. The book combined autobiographical elements with extensive exposition of his political ideology, including extreme German nationalism, antisemitism, and the concept of Lebensraum.

The putsch's failure led Hitler to revise his strategy. Rather than seizing power through force, he concluded that the Nazis must pursue power through electoral means, a strategy that succeeded when the Great Depression created conditions for the party's rapid electoral rise from the late 1920s onward.
